input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin system under study is the MABI MAX 100 industrial robot, specifically its first vertical axis. This serial robot comprises six axes, with the focus on the base axis for digital shadow creation. The operational domain is manufacturing, where precise motion control and friction modeling are critical. Key characteristics enabling digital twinning include the need for real-time monitoring of mechanical dynamics and multi-physics-aware friction identification to enhance control accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides services including continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction and optimization of robot motion control accuracy. These services target manufacturing engineers and control system developers, enabling data-driven refinement of robot performance through real-time monitoring and predictive adjustments based on friction modeling.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT are applied for system modeling across abstraction levels. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs define component-based structures with interfaces and interconnections. Behavior modeling uses MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines, integrated with MathWorks Simulink for simulation, and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) for detailed behavior. These tools collectively address heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives and system complexity.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Shadow system employs the reference model defined in Fig. 1 (UML class diagram), integrating SysML for functional architecture modeling (IBDs), Simulink for behavior simulation, and Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for data exchange and visualization.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Data exchange occurs via MQTT protocol for live measurements and Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for structured data representation and visualization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for structural modeling (including IBDs), Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc for component-and-connector architecture descriptions, and MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines for behavioral modeling. Analytical models are implemented using MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose code in C++ or Java. Data is sourced from physical system states via one-way data flow, with integrated management across heterogeneous tools and stakeholder perspectives as per MBSE methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data including input current and output angle are streamed from physical components to the digital shadow via MQTT protocol. The document explicitly states this communication method but does not specify transmission frequencies, event triggers, data quality assurance, preprocessing steps, or integration mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for sensor data streaming; no details on network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security protocols beyond the protocol usage are provided.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system adheres to industry standards including SysML for model-based systems engineering, ACME, AADL, and MontiArc as architecture description languages for component & connector perspectives, and methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for multi-level abstraction management. It integrates heterogeneous stakeholders through standardized modeling practices, utilizing tools like Simulink and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) for detailed behavior modeling and simulation.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the physical twin as the 'MABI MAX 100 industrial robot' with six axes, specifically focusing on the 'first vertical robot axis' for the digital shadow. It correctly identifies the operational domain as manufacturing and mentions the need for friction modeling and real-time monitoring. Quotes: 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'first vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital shadow', 'suitable for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining'.

The extraction correctly describes the core service as the 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction' to enhance motion accuracy. However, it speculates on 'predictive adjustments' and identifies specific target users ('manufacturing engineers and control system developers') which are not explicitly mentioned in the source text. The text focuses on 'identification' and 'understanding' rather than prediction. Quotes: 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction', 'enhance the robot’s motion accuracy'.

The extraction accurately summarizes MBSE methodologies, ADLs, and behavior modeling tools from the Background section. Quotes: 'There exist different methodologies on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP... OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT' and 'As main concepts, they describe systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc or SysML IBDs.' It also correctly mentions behavior modeling tools: 'Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in SysML v1.7... often combined with further (modeling) tools such as MathWorks Simulink... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java.' The extraction is detailed and well-supported, earning a score of 5.

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and

The characteristic description asks for these specific details, which are absent.

The extraction accurately describes the system's orchestration, citing the reference model in Fig. 1 (UML), the use of SysML for architecture (IBDs), Simulink for simulation, and AAS for data exchange and visualization. Quotes: 'Fig. 1 presents this refined version of the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) as a Unified Modeling Language (UML) class diagram', 'Fig. 3 shows the robot’s structure as a component & connector SysML model', 'Friction within the first axis is modeled with executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink', 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS), and live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard'.

The extraction correctly identifies MQTT for live measurements and AAS for structured data representation and visualization. Quotes: 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT', 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS), and live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard'.
